messages.author: Barry KO6JFI
messages.text: It seems like a denial-of-service attack is possible by sending out flood messages to random addresses, forcing repeaters to use up bandwidth trying to find a path to a client that doesn't exist. Are repeaters set up to throttle these kinds of DOS attacks?
messages.content_html: It seems like a denial-of-service attack is possible by sending out flood messages to random addresses, forcing repeaters to use up bandwidth trying to find a path to a client that doesn't exist. Are repeaters set up to throttle these kinds of DOS attacks?

messages.author: KA5PLE (Andy)
messages.text: That is actually a real concern here as well. At the very least a way for repeater operators to ban a certain node would be great to have. And at the same time a way to send a message to the repeater operators of distant repeaters so that we can limit the damage a real attack would do.
messages.content_html: That is actually a real concern here as well. At the very least a way for repeater operators to ban a certain node would be great to have. And at the same time a way to send a message to the repeater operators of distant repeaters so that we can limit the damage a real attack would do.

messages.author: Barry KO6JFI
messages.text: The sender could randomize their address for every packet. I think it would be very difficult to block this sort of attack.
messages.content_html: The sender could randomize their address for every packet. I think it would be very difficult to block this sort of attack.
messages.reactions_html: 2

messages.author: RQ (darco)
messages.text: Yes, it is hard to prevent. One way to possibly mitigate would be for repeaters to realize when they are the first hop of such an attack and start to automatically throttle themselves so that it doesn't take down the whole mesh.
messages.content_html: Yes, it is hard to prevent. One way to possibly mitigate would be for repeaters to realize when they are the first hop of such an attack and start to automatically throttle themselves so that it doesn't take down the whole mesh.

messages.author: retromesh
messages.text: It's only a matter of time unfortunately. I'm not sure there's enough compute power in these devices to have an effective security layer. I think the only chance is first packet validation (a crude implementation of zero trust, if you will) - but how is the question.
messages.content_html: It's only a matter of time unfortunately. I'm not sure there's enough compute power in these devices to have an effective security layer. I think the only chance is first packet validation (a crude implementation of zero trust, if you will) - but how is the question.

messages.author: Neil
messages.text: Difficult to do first-hop avoidance with limited memory, state that you keep about one node possibly leads to the eviction of state that you keep about another
messages.content_html: Difficult to do first-hop avoidance with limited memory, state that you keep about one node possibly leads to the eviction of state that you keep about another

messages.author: Theodoor
messages.text: Instead of trying to solve the problem on the local repeater, why not base it on network monitoring. As soon as a repeater starts flooding the network, just switch it off? Or is that to easy? (edited)
messages.content_html: Instead of trying to solve the problem on the local repeater, why not base it on network monitoring. As soon as a repeater starts flooding the network, just switch it off? Or is that to easy? (edited)
